explanation.text: The alarm, a parent's call, the bus ride, and the school start recreate a morning many students know. This familiar routine gives readers an experience they can connect to before the article explains teen sleep loss.

The sentence says that even an awake but tired driver has impaired concentration and coordination, making crashes more likely. In this context, impaired concentration means an inability to focus well.

Mindell calls sleep a basic biological need and explains that too little sleep weakens immune function and disrupts hormones. She also says tired people cannot regulate their emotions, showing effects on both body and mind.

“Taking a Toll” describes what sleep deprivation does, including poor grades, crashes, illness, and unstable moods. “So Why Don't Teens Sleep More?” then explains causes such as electronics, caffeine, and teens' natural sleep patterns.
The speaker observes that people brag about getting very little sleep but never brag about sleeping enough. Treating sleep loss as an achievement shows that people do not recognize its seriousness.

Wolfson's research links less sleep with C, D, and F grades rather than A's and B's. The same evidence says sleep loss makes people more likely to feel depressed, directly connecting more sleep with school success and happiness.
The survey connects heavy electronics use and daily caffeine consumption with insufficient sleep. Those findings support the tips to limit electronics before bed and avoid caffeine after lunchtime.

Before Project Sea Hunt, Jim had trained dolphins, whales, and gooney birds and had studied how animals behave and learn. That previous animal work demonstrates that he had relevant experience for leading the pigeon program.

The phrase appears while the pigeons' detection performance is measured against a human's performance. “Nose to beak” playfully parallels “head to head,” showing that the birds are being compared directly with people.

The program began with a large number of pigeons, yet only about twelve to fourteen completed ten months of training. That low completion rate shows how difficult it was to teach the required behavior reliably.

After training the pigeons to recognize rescue colors and press an alert button, Jim put them on official missions “to put their skills to the test.” The missions therefore assessed whether they could apply what they had learned.

The pigeons never located a missing person, but they did spot small objects in the exact colors they had learned to identify. That result shows the training worked in part even though the larger rescue goal was not achieved.

The pigeons' ability to see objects from nearly seven football fields away explains why they were valuable searchers. That central ability is more important to a summary than details about Jim's earlier animals or the cockpit signal.

The article's central focus is the surprising ability of trained pigeons to scan the ocean quickly, for long periods, and at great distance. Describing those searching abilities explains why pigeons could serve as rescue workers.

Because electricity has reached the farm, Ruby immediately asks Howard about getting an electric sewing machine. That decision launches the machine's decades of sewing and the later search for a replacement.

These lines compress years of Ruby's sewing into a long list of clothes, quilts, and special dresses for family and neighbors. The sheer amount and variety of her work demonstrate her lasting passion for sewing.

The working 1948 Singer is only a display and costs the narrator just twenty-five dollars, yet it restores the activity that gives Ruby purpose. This contrast shows that an object's importance can far exceed its price.
Ruby sews to stay busy and serves people who have little money, often charging almost nothing. Calling sewing her “ministry” therefore means it is a central purpose through which she helps others.

The narrator tells the shopkeeper that Ruby sews for poor neighbors, charges very little, and often works for free because sewing is her ministry. Choosing these generous details reveals his deep admiration for her.
After Ruby makes matching dresses, Rachael responds with thanks, love, and a wish to see her again. Because that affection directly answers Ruby's care, it is the meaningful reward for her sewing.

Ruby makes clothing and quilts for family, neighbors, brides, and children, often for little or no money. Each stitch becomes an act of care, so the phrase means she expresses kindness through what she sews.
